analogy: Think of "Poverty" through the chapter's own opening images: CURRENT CHALLENGES FACING THE INDIAN ECONOMY UNIT III UNIT III After studying this chapter, the learners will understand • the concepts of Human Resource, Human Capital Formation and Human Development • the links between investment in human capital, economic growth and human development • the... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: CURRENT CHALLENGES FACING THE, INDIAN ECONOMY, HUMAN CAPITAL, FORMATION IN INDIA. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Poverty is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that cURRENT CHALLENGES FACING THE INDIAN ECONOMY UNIT III UNIT III

The text explains that after studying this chapter, the learners will understand • the concepts of Human Resource, Human Capital Formation and Human Development • the links between investment in human capital, economic growth and human development • the need for government spending on education and health • the state of India’s educational attainment.

The text explains that 59 HUMAN CAPITAL FORMATION IN INDIA 4.1 INTRODUCTION Think of one factor that has made a great difference in the evolution of mankind.

The text explains that perhaps it is human capacity to store and transmit knowledge which they have been doing through conversation, through songs and through elaborate lectures.

The text explains that but humans soon found out that we need a good deal of training and skill to do things efficiently.

The text explains that we know that the labour skill of an educated person is more than that of an uneducated person and hence the former is able to generate more income than the latter and his or her contribution to economic growth is, consequently, more.

The text explains that education is sought not only as it confers higher earning capacity on people but also for its other highly valued benefits: it gives one a better social standing and pride; it enables one to make better choices in life; it provides knowledge to understand the changes taking place in society; it also stimulates innovations.

The text explains that moreover, the availability of educated labour force facilitates adaptation of new “... the wisdom of expending public and private funds on education is not to be measured by its direct fruits alone.

The text explains that it will be profitable as a mere investment, to give the masses of people much greater opportunities than they can generally avail themselves of.

The text explains that for by this means many, who would have died unknown, are enabled to get the start needed for bringing out their latent abilities”.

The text explains that 4.1 Adequate education and training to farmers can raise productivity in farms

The text explains that economists have stressed the need for expanding educational opportunities in a nation as it accelerates the development process.

The text explains that just as a country can turn physical resources like land into physical capital like factories, similarly, it can also turn human resources like nurses, farmers, teachers, students into human capital like engineers and doctors.

The text explains that societies need sufficient human capital in the first place — in the form of competent people who have themselves been educated and trained as professors and other professionals.

The text explains that in other words, we need good human capital to produce other human capital (say, nurses, farmers, teachers, doctors, engineers...).

The text explains that this means that we need investment in human capital to produce more human capital out of human resources.

The text explains that let us understand a little more of what human capital means by posing the following questions : (i) What are the sources of human capital?

The text explains that (iv) What role can the government play in human capital formation in India?

The text explains that 4.3 SOURCES OF HUMAN CAPITAL Investment in education is considered as one of the main sources of human capital.

The text explains that investments in health, on- the- job training, migration and information are the other sources of human capital formation.

The text explains that hence, expenditure on health is an important source of human capital formation.

The text explains that health expenditure directly increases the supply of healthy labour force and is, thus, a source of human capital formation.

The text explains that expenditure regarding on- the-job training is a source of human capital formation as the return of such expenditure in the form of enhanced labour productivity is more than the cost of it.

The text explains that unemployment is the reason for the rural-urban migration in India.

The text explains that the enhanced earnings in the new place outweigh the costs of migration; hence, expenditure on migration is also a source of human capital formation.

The text explains that for example, people want to know the level of salaries associated with various types of jobs, whether the educational institutions provide the right type of employable skills and at what cost.

The text explains that expenditure incurred for acquiring information relating to the labour market and other markets is also a source of human capital formation.

The text explains that the ownership of physical capital is the outcome of the conscious decision of the owner — the physical capital formation is mainly an economic and technical process.

The text explains that children are given different types of school education and health care facilities by their parents and the society.

The text explains that the peers, educators and society influence the decisions regarding human capital investments even at the tertiary level, that is, at the college level.

The text explains that human capital formation is partly a social process and partly a conscious decision of the possessor of the human capital.

The text explains that therefore, physical capital formation can be built even through imports, whereas human capital formation is to be done through conscious policy formulations in consonance with the nature of the society and economy and expenditure by the state and the individuals.

The text explains that human capital benefits not only the owner but also the society in general.

The text explains that an educated person can effectively take part in a democratic process and contribute to the socio-economic progress of a nation.

The text explains that education provides knowledge to understand changes in society and scientific advancements, thus, facilitate inventions and innovations.

The text explains that there are reasons to believe that the causality between human capital and economic growth flows in either directions.

The text explains that the Seventh Five Year Plan says, “Human resources development (read human capital) has necessarily to be assigned a key role in any development strategy, particularly in a country with a large population.

The text explains that trained and educated on sound lines, a large population can itself become an asset in accelerating economic growth and in ensuring social change in desired directions.” It is difficult to establish a relation of cause and effect from the growth of human capital (education and health) to economic growth but we can see in Fig.

The text explains that with climate change, increasing pollution, and depleting natural resources, there will be a sizeable shift in how we meet the world’s energy, water, food, and sanitation needs, again resulting in the need for new skilled labour, particularly in biology, chemistry, physics, agriculture, climate science, and social science.

The text explains that there will be a growing demand for humanities and art, as India moves towards becoming a developed country as well as TABLE 4.1 Select Indicators of Development in Education and Health Sectors Particulars 1951 1981 1991 2001 2018-22 Real Per Capita Income (in Rs) 7,651 12,174 15,748 23,095 94,054 Crude Death 25.1 12.5 9.8 8.1 6.0 Rate (Per 1,000 Population) Infant Mortality Rate 146 110 80 63 28 Life Expectancy 37.2 54.1 59.7 63.9 68.6 at Birth (in Years) 36.2 54.7 60.9 66.9 71.4 16.67 43.57 52.21 65.20 78 Female Male Literacy Rate (%) Source: Economic Survey for various years, Ministry of Finance; National Statistical Office, Ministry of Statistics and Programme Implementation, Government of India.

The text explains that 4.4 Job on hand: transforming India into a knowledge economy

The text explains that this policy vision suggests how human capital formation in India will move its economy to a higher growth trajectory based on knowledge landscape.

The text explains that in such a view, every individual has a right to get basic education and basic health care, that is, every individual has a right to be literate and lead a healthy life.

The text explains that box 4.2: India as a Knowledge Economy The Indian software industry has been showing an impressive record over the past two decades.

The text explains that entrepreneurs, bureaucrats and politicians are now advancing views about how India can transform itself into a knowledge-based economy by using information technology (IT).

The text explains that these can be downloaded from websites of the respective Union Government Ministries.

The text explains that 2nd Edition, Columbia University Press, New York.

The text explains that human Capital and Development — The Indian Experience.

The text explains that government Reports India Human Development Report 2011: Towards Social Inclusion, Plan- ning Commission, Government of India.

The text explains that educational Statistics At a glance, Ministry of Education (for various years), Government of India.

The text explains that annual Reports, Ministry of Education, Government of India.

The text explains that national Education Policy 2020, Ministry of Education, Govt. of India, New Delhi.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Indian Economic Development, Chapter 4: Poverty

prelims_traps: Do not treat CURRENT CHALLENGES FACING THE and INDIAN ECONOMY as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Poverty; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.

Use CURRENT CHALLENGES FACING THE and INDIAN ECONOMY to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
